message: Node started: Algorithmic Blueprint: Tree Merging Transformation 1. Core Strategy: The transformation is a process of reduction. Since we can only merge siblings, the parent-child relationships must be preserved or condensed. A node in the final tree corresponds to a set of nodes in the initial tree that share the same parent and are merged together. The key is to identify which initial nodes are survivors and which are merged. 2. Data Structures: Adjacency lists for both the initial tree and the final tree; A mapping or lookup table to identify the parent of any given node; A way to track the current state of the initial tree. 3. Logical Steps for Transformation: Step A: Structural Alignment. For every node in the final tree, identify its corresponding set of children in the initial tree. A node in the final tree must be the maximum value among the set of initial nodes that were merged to form it. Step B: Identify Merges. For each parent in the initial tree, compare the set of children in the initial tree with the set of children in the final tree. If a node is a child in the initial tree but not in the final tree, it must have been merged into a sibling s where s > v. Step C: Validation of Merges. A merge between sibling v and sibling s is valid if s is the node that eventually represents the combined subtree in the final tree. Step D: Execution Order. Iterate through each parent in the initial tree and, for all children in the initial tree that are not in the final tree child set for that parent, merge them into the largest available sibling that is part of the final tree child set. 4. Precise Merging Logic: For each node p in the initial tree: Let S_initial be the set of children of p in the initial tree. Let S_final be the set of children of p in the final tree. For every node v in S_initial that is NOT in S_final: Find the node target in S_final such that v is part of the group that forms target.
